questions.question: When is fine-tuning the right choice?
options: When prompting and RAG fail -- custom style, persistent format, domain-specific knowledge at scale
When the model is too large
questions.explanation: Hierarchy: prompting first (free) -> RAG (cheap) -> fine-tuning (medium cost/effort) -> pre-training (very expensive).

questions.question: LoRA reduces trainable parameters by:
options: Adding low-rank matrices B*A beside frozen pre-trained weights W
questions.explanation: W_update = B*A where B(m,r) and A(r,n), r<<min(m,n). Only B and A train -- 0.1-1% of original parameters. W stays frozen.

questions.question: The rank 'r' in LoRA controls:
options: Size of the low-rank update -- higher r = more expressive but more parameters
questions.explanation: r=4-16 typical. Higher r captures more complex adaptations. Lower r = more compression, less forgetting of pre-trained knowledge.

questions.question: QLoRA enables fine-tuning large models on consumer GPUs by:
options: Combining 4-bit quantised base weights with LoRA adapters -- 4x memory reduction
questions.explanation: QLoRA: load 70B model in 4-bit (35GB instead of 140GB), add LoRA adapters in float16. Enables fine-tuning on A100 instead of 8xA100.

questions.question: Instruction tuning uses training examples formatted as:
options: (instruction, input, output) triples -- teaching models to follow diverse instructions
questions.explanation: Alpaca format: ### Instruction, ### Input, ### Response. Taught GPT-4-style instruction following to open models like LLaMA.

questions.question: Catastrophic forgetting in fine-tuning means:
options: The model loses general capabilities while gaining task-specific ones
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning on narrow data overwrites general knowledge. LoRA mitigates this by keeping W frozen and only adding small update matrices.

questions.question: PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning) includes which methods?
options: LoRA, Prefix Tuning, Prompt Tuning, Adapters
questions.explanation: PEFT umbrella: LoRA (low-rank), prefix tuning (learn virtual tokens), adapters (small modules). All update <1% of parameters.

questions.question: The learning rate for fine-tuning should be:
options: Much lower than pre-training -- typically 1e-5 to 2e-4
questions.explanation: High LR overwrites pre-trained knowledge (catastrophic forgetting). Low LR makes small, careful updates while preserving general capability.

questions.question: DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation) is an alternative to RLHF that:
options: Requires a reward model and PPO training
Directly optimises on preference pairs (chosen vs rejected) without a separate reward model
questions.explanation: DPO removes the separate reward model step, training directly on preference pairs -- simpler, more stable, comparable alignment results.

questions.question: After fine-tuning, the LoRA adapter is typically:
options: Merged back into the base model weights for inference efficiency
questions.explanation: adapter_merged = W_base + B*A. Merge = single model, no extra adapter layers at inference. Can also keep separate for hot-swapping multiple tasks.
